summary: Gruber catalogs the numerous problems with Apple's just-launched iTunes ringtone feature, from buggy ringtone-eligibility detection to inconsistent behavior across systems. He finds the error messages weaselly and dishonest, particularly the dialog that deflects when users try to make ringtones from non-iTunes Store tracks. He also dissects the absurd Terms of Service language, which technically prohibits using purchased ringtones as alarm sounds even though Apple's own Clock app encourages exactly that. The piece builds a case that the entire ringtone system is a user-hostile mess driven by music label restrictions and legal overcaution.
key_points: The ringtone eligibility system is broken: songs marked as non-ringtone-able can sometimes be purchased as ringtones anyway, while the eligibility icons don't display correctly across systems
iTunes's error dialogs use deliberately evasive language to avoid admitting that non-iTunes Store tracks are completely locked out of the ringtone feature
A simple file extension renaming trick or free third-party utility bypasses the entire restriction for DRM-free files, making the official system look even more absurd
The Terms of Service contain contradictory restrictions — prohibiting ringtone use outside phone calls while Apple's own Clock app actively offers ringtones as alarm sounds
Charging $1 extra to turn an already-purchased song into a ringtone, then restricting how that ringtone can be used, is preposterous
money_quotes: Look, we know it sucks that only some of the songs in the iTunes Store are available as ringtones. Trust us, we'd love to sell them all to you as ringtones. Don't blame us, blame the dopes at the music labels.
Think about how weaselly this wording is. It's like a politician who's asked a pointed question about topic A, and responds with a non sequitur talking point regarding topic B.
Perhaps whoever wrote the copy for this dialog was simply too ashamed to spell it out: iTunes will not allow you to use any non-iTunes Store audio file with its built-in Create Ringtone feature.
You've paid $1 for the song, you've paid another $1 to turn the song you already paid for into a ringtone, and you're not allowed to use the ringtone in any other context? Preposterous.
Clock actively encourages you to break this rule from the Terms of Service.
Someone needs to tell Apple Legal that it's ringtone, not ring tone.
key_insight: Apple's ringtone feature is a case study in how music industry restrictions produce a user experience riddled with bugs, dishonest UI copy, and Terms of Service that contradict Apple's own software.
